rationale: The citation is not just a name-drop in a long list: the authors explicitly explain what Yang and Allenby (2003) does (interdependent utilities via cross-consumer error correlation) and compare it to their own residual-sharing approach. They use it to position their contribution by contrasting geographic similarity in the cited work with preference-similarity weighting in their model. Although it appears in the discussion/future-research section rather than the core estimation steps, it still plays a meaningful conceptual positioning role.
relation_description: The citing paper conceptually positions its virtual-expert model against Yang and Allenby (2003) as a related interdependence framework, highlighting similarities in modeling cross-consumer dependence and differences in how similarity is defined. It frames the cited model as a complementary line that could be combined with the current approach in future work.
